coredata.dc:title: N-doped multi-fluorescent carbon dots for ‘turn off-on’ silver-biothiol dual sensing and mammalian cell imaging application
coredata.dc:creator: Borse, Vivek
Thakur, Mukeshchand
Sengupta, Sohini
Srivastava, Rohit
coredata.dc:description: Bright multi-fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized using prolonged time microwave-assisted heating (PT-MAH) method. C-dots were found to be spherical, monodispersed, uniformly distributed with the ultra-small size of about 3.3±0.4nm. The C-dots showed high oxygen content and nitrogen-doping (N-doped), thereby elucidating a competitive quantum yield (QY: 17.73%). The presence of silver ions (Ag+) in drinking water needs to be monitored to prevent related human health hazards. Biothiols especially glutathione (GSH) plays a vital role in numerous biological processes. Alteration of GSH concentration from normal level is observed in various diseases and disorders. We demonstrate ‘turn-off’ dynamic fluorescence-based sensing of Ag+ ions and ‘turn-on’ fluorescence-based on sensing of GSH using C-dots as dual-sensor. Wide range concentrations of Ag+ ions (1–1000μM) were detected when Ag+ ions were spiked in C-dots suspended in three representative solutions: Milli-Q, purifier and tap water. The addition of GSH (1–200μM) to the C-dots-Ag+ complex recovered fluorescence in linear correlation with good recovery. Furthermore, the C-dots showed excitation-dependent fluorescence, which was utilised for multicolor in vitro cellular imaging in fibroblasts. The C-dots showed high cytocompatibility and no deterring effect on cellular morphology. Thus, this study illustrates the potential of C-dots utility for silver and biothiol dual sensing and applicability intracellular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
‘Turn off-on’ sensing
Silver
Thiol
Bioimaging
Biocompatibility

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst otherkwds primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N-dope C-dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV–vis absorption spectra of synthesized C-dots; inset shows C-dots solution under day light and under UV lamp (excitation wavelength of 370nm). b) Excitation dependent fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots. Feasibility analysis of C-dots showing c) the effect of pH on fluorescence emission of C-dots (excitation wavelength 375nm) and d) photostability of C-dots studied by excitation/emission at 375/440nm for continuous time period of 15min.

High-resolution TEM images of the C-dots. a) Dispersed C-dots at scale bar: 10nm, b) Particle diameter size distribution, c) Individual C-dots at scale bar: 5nm with d) a magnified view of a C-dot showing lattice spacing.
Atomic and molecular functional group analysis of C-dots using a) XRD and b) FTIR analysis.
An XPS analysis of C-dots showing a) survey spectrum and deconvoluted high-resolution spectra of b) C1s, c) N1s and d) O1s.
Schematic illustration of the C-dots based ‘turn off-on’ silver-biothiol dual sensing.

Left panel shows fluorescence emission spectra of the C-dots suspended in a) Milli-Q water, c) purifier water and e) tap water with addition of different concentrations of Ag+. Right panel shows plots of % quenching against different concentrations of Ag+ for b) Milli-Q water, d) purifier water and f) tap water.

Left panel shows fluorescence emission spectra of the C-dots suspended in a) Milli-Q water, c) purifier water and e) tap water with addition of different concentrations of GSH to the previously quenched C-dots-Ag+ complex. Right panel shows plots of percentage recovery against different concentrations of GSH for b) Milli-Q water, d) purifier water and f) tap water.

a) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve for turn-off and turn-on sensing of C-dots using Ag+ and GSH respectively (inset showing fluorescence quenching and recovery of C-dots after addition of Ag+ and GSH, respectively). b) Fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots suspended in Milli-Q water with addition of different concentrations of GSH. c) Effect of the various cations on the fluorescence quenching of C-dots suspended in Milli-Q water.

Confocal microscopic imaging of L929 cells incubated with multi-fluorescent C-dots (100μg/mL); a) Differential interference contrast showing good fibroblast morphology, and fluorescence images taken at different excitation wavelengths b) 405nm, c) 488nm, and d) 543nm.

Cell viability analysis of L929 cells after 24h of treatment with various concentrations of C-dots using an MTT assay. Negative control is untreated cells whereas a positive control involves triton-X100 (10μM). Readings were taken as n =3, mean±SD.
Kinetic parameters for fluorescence lifetime measurements.

head.ce:title: N-doped multi-fluorescent carbon dots for ‘turn off-on’ silver-biothiol dual sensing and mammalian cell imaging application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Bombay, Powai, Mumbai 400076, Maharashtra, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Bombay

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silver-biothiol dual sensing – reaction of Ag+ with C-dots ‘turn-off’ while addition of GSH ‘turn-on’ the fluorescence of C-dots.

Bright multi-fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized using prolonged time microwave-assisted heating (PT-MAH) method. C-dots were found to be spherical, monodispersed, uniformly distributed with the ultra-small size of about 3.3±0.4nm. The C-dots showed high oxygen content and nitrogen-doping (N-doped), thereby elucidating a competitive quantum yield (QY: 17.73%). The presence of silver ions (Ag+) in drinking water needs to be monitored to prevent related human health hazards. Biothiols especially glutathione (GSH) plays a vital role in numerous biological processes. Alteration of GSH concentration from normal level is observed in various diseases and disorders. We demonstrate ‘turn-off’ dynamic fluorescence-based sensing of Ag+ ions and ‘turn-on’ fluorescence-based on sensing of GSH using C-dots as dual-sensor. Wide range concentrations of Ag+ ions (1–1000μM) were detected when Ag+ ions were spiked in C-dots suspended in three representative solutions: Milli-Q, purifier and tap water. The addition of GSH (1–200μM) to the C-dots-Ag+ complex recovered fluorescence in linear correlation with good recovery. Furthermore, the C-dots showed excitation-dependent fluorescence, which was utilised for multicolor in vitro cellular imaging in fibroblasts. The C-dots showed high cytocompatibility and no deterring effect on cellular morphology. Thus, this study illustrates the potential of C-dots utility for silver and biothiol dual sensing and applicability intracellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: C-dots are a new type of carbon nanomaterial that possesses exciting optical and photophysical properties [1,2]. Recently, a number of researchers have explored physical, chemical and biological properties of C-dots, particularly in the field of analyte sensing and biological imaging [3–6]. C-dots are the choice of sensing probes owing to its unique characteristics such as ultra-small size, aqueous solubility, tunable photoluminescence, non-blinking fluorescence, photostability, and biocompatibility [6,7]. The most important property among these are photoluminescence of C-dots, showing excitation dependent as well as independent emission property, multicolor emission, photostable, and up-conversion as well as down-conversion fluorescence. These properties are attributed to quantum confinement, surface energy traps, and defects in the structure of C-dots [8,9]. Amongst various top down and bottom up approaches for the synthesis of C-dots, microwave-assisted heating (MAH) found to be advantageous because of easy methodology, solvent-free reaction, non-hazardous, eco-friendly, producing high quantum yield (QY) C-dots, etc. The MAH method can be tuned for the synthesis of various sized C-dots, which have majorly been used for biolabeling of mammalian or bacterial cells and detection of metal ions. [5,7,10,11]. Many research groups have reported the advantages of doping on intrinsic photoluminescence property of C-dots [12,13]. For instance, N-doping causes significant enhancement in the QY of C-dots due to quantum confinement and edge effect of the nitrogen atoms [14,15]. Thus, carbon precursors such as thiourea, organo-sulfur compound can provide inherent N-doping characteristics in C-dots and PEGylation can increase the solubility and QY of C-dots [13,16,2].

Pure and safe drinking water is a very important aspect of human health. Nowadays, numerous commercialized water purifiers utilize silver as an antimicrobial as well as an antifungal agent. There is often a possibility of leaching of silver ions over the period of time in the system contaminating the water supply. The presence of toxic heavy metal ions in drinking water is responsible for allergic reactions, diarrhea, chronic abnormalities, growth retardation, and cancer; depending upon the concentration of toxins and its properties, it may also affect the kidney or nervous system's functions [17,18]. Biological thiols i.e. cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), and glutathione (GSH) play a major role in physiological processes by redox balance regulation [19]. Alteration in GSH metabolism and homoeostasis is observed in life-threatening consequences such as cancer, HIV infection, cystic fibrosis, neurodegenerative diseases and ageing [20]. Many spectroscopic tools such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), and X-ray fluorescence analysis has been utilized for detection of metal ions. However, these methods have major limitations such as the need for expensive instruments, highly-trained technicians, detect single ion at a time, and longer sample preparation protocols. In this regard, a cheap, simple, rapid, and label-free ion detection assay is highly desirable for detection of toxic ions in drinking water. Nanomaterials such as metallic gold [21] and silver [22], or semiconductor quantum dots [23], graphene quantum dots [14], carbon dots [24–28], etc. have been used in past for detection of ionic components in analytes. Recently, graphene quantum dots were used for intracellular temperature sensing and photothermal/photodynamic applications in collaboration with imaging module in mammalian cells [29,30]. Thus, graphene-based sensors (including C-dots) are the recent focus for metal ion sensing in water due to their small size, ease of fabrication, large surface-to-area ratio, oxygen-rich surface groups, and their in situ real-time measurements [6,7,31].

In this article, we report the synthesis of C-dots using hydrothermal prolonged time microwave-assisted heating (PT-MAH) method. The C-dots were used to ‘turn on-off’ silver-thiol dual sensing in Milli-Q, commercial purifier, and a tap water. The addition of Ag+ ions causes fluorescence quenching of C-dots, while the addition of GSH to the same reaction mixture recovers fluorescence intensity. The concentration dependent percent quenching and recovery were calculated. Cellular imaging, as well as biocompatibility studies, have been carried out in mammalian fibroblast cells, which indicated the utility of the C-dots for cell imaging and various biomedical applications.

Polyethylene glycol (PEG, OH(C2H4O)nH, 20,000g/mol), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O, 203.30g/mol, >99%), manganese sulfate monohydrate (MnSO4·H2O, 169.01g/mol, >99%) and glutathione (GSH, C10H17N3O6S, 307.32g/mol, >99.5%) were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. India. Urea (CH4N2O, 60.06g/mol, >99%), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 270.33g/mol, >99%), copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O, 249.68g/mol, >99%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 169.87g/mol, >99%), mercury (II) chloride (HgCl2, 271.5g/mol, ≥ 99.5%), lead (II) chloride (PbCl2, 278.1g/mol, ≥ 98.0%), cadmium chloride monohydrate (CdCl2·H2O, 201.32g/mol, ≥98%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 58.44g/mol, ≥99.5%), potassium chloride (KCl, 74.55g/mol, ≥99.5%), cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2, 6H2O, 237.90g/mol, >99%) and zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4·7H2O, 287.54g/mol, >99.5%) were purchased from Merck, India. Quinine sulfate dihydrate (C40H48N4O4·H2SO4·2H2O, 782.96g/mol, 99.5%) was purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd., India. Dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO, C2H6OS, 78.13g/mol, 99%) was purchased from Fisher Scientific, India. 3-(4, 5 dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) dye was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. Dulbecco's modified Eagles Medium (DMEM) and dialysis bag (MW 1kD cut-off) were purchased from HiMedia, India. Ultrapure (Milli-Q) water (pH 6.5) from Milli-Q water system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA), purifier water (pH 6.7) from the Aquasure water purifier (Eureka Forbes, India) and tap water (pH 6.8) supplied by Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC, Mumbai, India) was used for experiments. Mouse fibroblasts L929 cells were procured from National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India.

Hydrothermal PT-MAH method was used for synthesis of C-dots with certain modifications in the process reported by Zhu et al. [32]. Briefly, 2.5g urea was added to the Milli-Q water (50mL, pH 6.5) with continuous stirring at room temperature. About 2.5g PEG was added to above solution and stirring was continued until a clear solution was formed. The reaction mixture was heated in a domestic microwave oven for 90min (Parameters: 180W, 20% power). A Milli-Q water was added to the dark yellow colored thick paste, which forms after microwave heating process. The C-dots were purified by centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 10min to remove residual carbon nanoparticles and the supernatant was subjected to dialysis (MW 1kD cut-off) for 18h. Fluorescence of the transparent pale yellow colored solution obtained from dialysis bag was observed under UV lamp (370nm). The aqueous solution or lyophilized powder of C-dots was at 4°C for further experiments.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the C-dots were recorded using Lambda 25 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, India). Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded using F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) using path length of 1cm. The morphology and microstructure of C-dots were analyzed using high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) with accelerating voltage 200kV (Jeol JEM-2100F, USA). A drop of aqueous C-dots solution was placed on the carbon-coated copper grid and was air dried before HRTEM analysis. Zeta potential analysis of diluted aqueous C-dots solution was performed using zeta analyzer ZetaPALS (Brookhaven Instruments Co., USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed by preparing a thin film of C-dots solution on an aluminium foil with AXIS Supra, Kratos Analytical instrument (Shimadzu, UK). Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed to analyze the functional groups present on the surface of C-dots using 3000 Hyperion Microscope with Vertex 80 FTIR System (Bruker, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using powder diffractometer PW3040/60 (Philips). The CHNSO (Carbon-Hydrogen-Nitrogen-Sulfur-Oxygen) analysis was performed on FLASH EA 1112 series (Thermo Finnigan, Italy). Fluorescence emission intensity based sensing of silver ions and GSH was carried out using multifunctional microplate reader Infinite M200 Pro (Tecan, USA). Cellular imaging was performed using confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Axio-Observer Z1 microscope, Zeiss, Germany). Fluorescence lifetime decay study was performed using Simple-Tau instrument (Becker and Hickl Gmbh–High-Performance Photon Counting, Germany). Fluorescence lifetime decay was calculated after processing the data using Single Photon Counting software (SPC-150).

QY of the C-dots was calculated by using a comparative method [33]. Quinine sulphate dissolved in sulfuric acid (ϕ st =54%) was used as a standard for QY determination [34]. The dilutions were prepared using Milli-Q water. The absorbance was measured at 350nm using UV–vis spectrophotometer and optical density values were kept below 0.1 to reduce the reabsorption effect. Fluorescence emission spectra of quinine sulphate, as well as C-dots, were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350nm. A graph was plotted for integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance. The QY of C-dots was calculated using the following equation [33],
ϕ x
=
ϕ
s t
G r a
d x
G r a
d
s t
η x
η
s t

where ϕ is QY, subscripts x and st represents test and standard respectively, Grad is the slope of fluorescence intensity versus absorbance plot, η is the refractive index of the solvent (η =1.33 for water as well as sulfuric acid).

Different concentrations of Ag+ ions ranging from 1 to1000μM were prepared in Milli-Q water (pH 6.5). Sensing experiments were carried out in a 96-well plate where C-dots solution (100μg/mL) was used as a control and a total volume of 100μL (with or without ions). Fluorescence readings were recorded using plate reader after 10min at an excitation wavelength of 320nm and emission wavelength of 350–450nm. Similar sensing experiments were repeated for purifier and tap water. Percent fluorescence quenching was calculated using the following equation,
% Q u e n c h i n g =
F 0
−
F Q
F 0
× 100

where F
0 is fluorescence of the C-dots in water (control) and F
Q is the quenched fluorescence of the C-dots after addition of the Ag+ ions.

For fluorescence recovery analysis, GSH (10μL; 1 – 200μM) was added to the C-dot-Ag+ complex reaction mixture to study the fluorescence recovery. The reaction mixture was mixed thoroughly and fluorescence was measured using plate reader after 10min at the excitation wavelength of 320nm and emission in the range of 350–450nm. Percent fluorescence recovery was calculated using following equation,
% R e c o v e r y =
F R
F 0
× 100

where F
R is recovered fluorescence of the C-dots after addition of the GSH and F
0 is fluorescence of the C-dots in water (control).

Water may consist of various ions as contaminants or impurities, thus to negate the possibility of false positive results, the effect of various ions (especially cations) on the Ag+ ions sensing was investigated. Aqueous solutions of 10 different salts (Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Zn2+) were prepared. Ionic concentration of 1000μM was chosen for all the salts to assess interference in sensing. Ten microliters of salt solution were added to 90μL of C-dots solution (100μg/mL), and the reaction mixture was mixed thoroughly. Change in fluorescence was measured using plate reader after 10min at an excitation wavelength of 320nm and emission wavelength of 350–450nm.

Cellular imaging has been studied by fluorescence imaging of L929 cells incubated with C-dots. The L929 cells were seeded on a sterile glass coverslip (4000 cells in DMEM media per well) in the 12-well plate. After 24h of cell attachment, cells were incubated with C-dots (100μg/mL) for 2h. Cell fixation was performed by treatment with paraformaldehyde (2.7% in PBS. pH 7) for 5min. Cells were washed thoroughly using PBS (pH 7.0) and fluorescence imaging was performed using CLSM at different excitation wavelengths, i.e., blue (405nm), green (488nm) and red (543nm).

To evaluate the biocompatibility of synthesized C-dots, an MTT assay was performed [35]. The L929 cells were cultured in DMEM and maintained at 5% CO2 and 37°C. Cells were seeded at a concentration of 5000 cells/well in 96-well plate. Various concentration of C-dots (1, 10, 100, 250 and 500μg/mL) were added to the cells and incubated for 24h. The MTT dye (20μL; 5mg/mL) was added to each well and incubated for 4h. The medium was aspirated and formazan crystals were dissolved by addition of DMSO (200μL) to each well. After 30min, absorbance was measured at 590nm with 620nm as a reference using plate reader. Percent cell viability was calculated using the following equation,
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
T e s t
C o n t r o l
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N-dope C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The C-dots synthesis was performed by a hydrothermal method in which urea and PEG were used in the ratio of 1:1, where urea is a source for carbon and nitrogen while PEG is a source for carbon and act as a passivating agent. The PT-MAH method has been proved to be efficient, cost effective, eco-friendly and produce monodispersed particles due to homogenous heating [36]. Surface passivation of C-dots by PEG aids in surface energy stabilization and impact the fluorescence emissive properties [34]. The aqueous solution of C-dots appears pale yellow color, which emits greenish blue color fluorescence under UV lamp (370nm) as displayed in the inset of Fig. 1a. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of the C-dots is represented in the Fig. 1a. Absorption spectrum shows wide absorption, which may be due to the broad size distribution of synthesized C-dots [33]. The absorbance peak at 237nm represents π-π* transition of the CC bond (sp2 domains) and shoulder peak at 300nm may be attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO band [37]. Fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots was measured at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 260 to 380nm.

Fig. 1b depicts that with an increase in excitation wavelength from 260 to 300nm, there is a shift in the fluorescence emission maxima towards shorter wavelength, while from excitation wavelength from 320 to 380nm, and it is shifted toward longer wavelength in non-uniform style along with significant decrease in intensity. An aqueous solution of C-dots exhibited characteristic peak emission intensity at 380nm at an excitation wavelength of 320nm. The C-dots showed excitation dependent fluorescence emission due to the association of different energy levels with various surface states formed by corresponding functional groups [4]. Higher emission intensities may be contributed by relatively well passivated but non-uniform C-dots surface [34]. In order to use C-dots as a sensor, its feasibility analysis should be evaluated so that the sensor could function at various physicochemical environments. Thus, we studied the effect of pH on the fluorescence property of C-dots (Fig. 1c). There was no shift in the fluorescence emission but minor changes in the intensity, which are due to the weak ionic changes on the surface of C-dots because of changes in the pH. Furthermore, we studied the effect of prolonged exposure of light excitation on the fluorescence emission of C-dots (Fig. 1d). The C-dots showed fairly stable fluorescence emission under continuous excitation for 15min. Thus, the C-dots would be feasible over a range of the pH of the solution and possess good photostability.

The morphological characteristics of synthesized C-dots were observed by HRTEM analysis. Fig. 2 a–c shows the spherical shaped, monodispersed and uniformly distributed C-dots. The average size of C-dots was measured to about 3.3±0.4nm (Fig. 2b) which is comparable to the previous reports [10,27,28,38–40]. The lattice spacing of 0.34nm is clearly visible in the Fig. 2d is assigned to [003] plane of graphitic carbon which represents crystallinity in the C-dots [39]. Structural properties of C-dots were confirmed by an XRD analysis. Fig. 3a shows a broad peak between 19 and 27°, along with sharp peaks at 21.94, 25.76 and 27.49̊. It can be concluded that synthesized C-dots contain both crystallines as well as amorphous phases of carbon [41–43]. An FTIR spectroscopy was carried out to identify various functional groups present in synthesized C-dots. As represented in Fig. 3b, characteristic absorption peaks were observed at 3436 and 2874cm−1 corresponding to the OH stretching and CH bending vibrations, respectively. Absorption peaks at 1692, 1621 and 1332cm−1 corresponds to CO, CC vibrations and symmetric COO stretching, respectively. The peaks at 1248 and 1077cm−1 are attributed to the presence of CO and COC stretching vibrations [33,38]. Zeta potential of aqueous C-dots solution was found to be −22.31±3.24mV, which shows the presence of negatively charged carboxyl functional group on the surface.

Furthermore, surface components of C-dots were investigated using an XPS analysis. Fig. 4a depicts XPS data, where three main peaks were observed at 285.24, 400.26 and 531.91eV corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. Upon deconvolution, C1s spectrum showed three peaks at 284.81, 285.93 and 289.42eV which corresponds to CC, CN and CO, respectively (Fig. 4b). Characteristic N-doping was confirmed and deconvolution of the N1s spectrum represents two peaks around 398.43 and 400.16eV corresponding to CNC and NC bonds, respectively (Fig. 4c). The O1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) was deconvoluted to two major peaks at 531.45 and 532.37eV corresponding to CO and COH/COC, respectively [11]. Elemental analysis (CHNSO) was carried out to find the core elemental components of C-dots. The CHNSO analysis showed that C-dots constitute majorly of carbon (77.80%) followed by oxygen (8.14%), hydrogen (3.02%), nitrogen (0.46%) and sulfur (0.27%). The QY of synthesized C-dots was found to be 17.73%, which is much brighter than the other reported methods [11,33,34,38].

Aqueous media with pH ranging between 6.5 and 6.8 was used for sensing reaction and final reaction volume was set to 100μL. Feasibility of sensing reaction was tested by adding 10μL of varying concentration of the Ag+ analyte (1–1000μM) to 90μL C-dots solution to keep the final C-dots concentration (100μg/ml). Fluorescence measurements showed detection ability of the reported method; it depicted reliable results for 100μL reaction volume and detected varying concentrations of Ag+.

Fig. 5 illustrates mechanism involved in the ‘turn off-on’ silver-biothiol dual sensing using C-dots. On addition of Ag+ ions, the cation binds to the C-dots forming a C-dots-Ag+ complex that results in fluorescence quenching, termed as ‘turn-off’ sensing. Biothiol (GSH) added to the C-dots-Ag+ complex reverse the quenching process by removing Ag+ ions bound on C-dots surface, this process of fluorescence recovery is termed as ‘turn-on’ sensing. Detection of Ag+ ions in three types of water samples i.e. water from Milli-Q system, purifier and tap water was performed. C-dots suspended in the respective type of water samples were used as a control for silver sensing experiments, fluorescence emission was observed at 380nm (excitation wavelength of 320nm). Fluorescence intensity versus emission wavelength graph was plotted to observe the changes in the intensity of C-dots upon addition of Ag+ ions. The left panel of Fig. 6 shows a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity of the C-dots solution with the addition of increasing concentration of Ag+ ions; a broad range of Ag+ concentrations from 1 to 1000μM (i.e. 1, 10, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500, 750 and 1000μM). The right panel of Fig. 6 shows a plot of percent quenching against the concentration of Ag+ ions, the inset shows enlarged concentration range (1–100μM) plot. In the case of C-dots suspended in Milli-Q water sample, a linear increase in percent quenching was observed upon addition of 1 to 100μM Ag+ ions concentration with R2 =0.969 (Figs. 6a and b). For 100μM Ag+ ions concentration, 51.31% quenching was observed. Beyond 100μM, percent quenching was reduced and saturation was observed at around 1000μM Ag+ ions concentration. The maximum quenching was found to be 72.33% for 1000μM Ag+ ions concentration. For C-dots suspended in purified water, a good linear correlation was found between percent quenching and the addition of Ag+ ions (R2 =0.934). Nearly, 59.72% quenching was observed upon addition of 100μM Ag+ ions concentration, while it was saturated to 81.76% for 1000μM, showing reduced quenching rate (Figs. 6c and d). For C-dots suspended in tap water sample, the addition of Ag+ ions from 1 to 100μM showed an increase in percent quenching with linear correlation (R2 =0.9229). The addition of 100μM Ag+ ions showed 59.73% quenching which was saturated to 85.57% for 1000μM (Figs. 6e and f). Limit of detection was calculated to determine the sensitivity of the method. The detection limit was calculated by statistical treatment of calibration curve; 30 times the standard deviation of blank was entered into the equation as the value of Y and corresponding X was calculated [45]. Limit of detection was found to be approximately 10nM for silver sensing in various types of water specimen which is less as compared to previous reports 200nM [28] and 168nM [45]. The lifetime decay was processed using SPC-150 software and data fitted to triple exponential function. Reduced chi-square method was applied to judge the quality of data fitting. Average lifetime was calculated using the following equation,
τ
a v g
= Σ
α i
τ i 2
/ Σ
α i
τ i

where α
i is pre-exponential factors and τ
i is lifetimes obtained in the triple exponential data fitting. The fluorescence lifetime of the C-dots was found to be 4.2ns, while it was reduced to 1.6ns after addition of Ag+ (1000μM). This supports the dynamic fluorescence quenching mechanism via non-radiative electron transfer process [44]. In C-dots aqueous suspension containing Ag+ ions, the cations bind to the negatively charged carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups present on the surface of C-dots forming temporary complexes. This hybridization process produces a C-dots-Ag+ complex that brings C-dots in close proximity to each other facilitating non-radiative excitons recombination via effective electron transfer process [46] which is evident from a decrease in the average lifetime of C-dots. This process results in a reduction in fluorescence emission of C-dots. From the silver sensing experiments, it is observed that fluorescence quenching pattern of C-dots suspended in a different type of water samples represents similar results, which explains reliability of sensitivity and specificity of C-dots for detection of wide range concentrations of Ag+ ions present in various analytes.

C-dots could be used for dual sensing application; the indication of this application was established by using the C-dots-Ag+ complex for biothiol (GSH) detection. The addition of GSH to fluorescence quenched reaction mixture (C-dots-Ag+ complex) results in the recovery of fluorescence. Fig. 7 left panel shows recovery of fluorescence intensity upon addition of different concentrations of GSH to C-dots-Ag+ complex reaction mixture. Fig. 7 right panel shows plots of percent recovery against the concentration of GSH. The addition of GSH to the C-dots-Ag+ complex reaction mixture in Milli-Q water (used as a control) showed an increase in percent fluorescence recovery with an increase in the concentration of GSH (Figs. 7a and b). Nearly 97% fluorescence recovery was observed upon addition of 200μM of GSH. Similar results were obtained on addition of GSH to C-dots-Ag+ complex reaction mixture in purifier and tap (Figs. 7c–f). The percent fluorescence recovery upon addition of 200μM of GSH was found to be 98.87 and 96.12% for purifier and tap water, respectively. A good linear fit was obtained between percent fluorescence recovery and GSH concentration ranging from one to 200μM (for a reaction carried out in Milli-Q, R2 =0.9831; for purified water, R2 =9856 and for tap water, R2 =9725). Fluorescence lifetime decay curve was derived for C-dots, C-dots-Ag+ complex and C-dots-Ag+-GSH solution (Fig. 8a). There was a decrease in fluorescence lifetime of C-dots upon addition of Ag+ ions (Turn OFF) and it recovered upon addition of GSH (Turn ON) (See Table 1 for fluorescence lifetime values). No significant change in fluorescence emission intensity was observed as compared to the control when different concentrations of GSH were added to the C-dots suspended in Milli-Q (control) (Fig. 8b). Limit of detection was found to be approximately 10nM for glutathione in various water specimens. Fluorescence lifetime was increased from 1.6 to 3.1ns when GSH was added to Ag+ quenched C-dots. Fluorescence lifetime measurement parameters are depicted in Table 1. The mechanism for fluorescence recovery could be attributed to the removal of Ag+ ions bound on the C-dots surface on the addition of GSH; thus unbound C-dots regains fluorescence (Fig. 5).

The selective binding reaction between C-dots and Ag+ ions was evaluated to confirm the detection ability and use of C-dots for dual sensing. Fig. 8c represents the effect of the addition of various cations on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots suspended in Milli-Q water (used as a control). No significant alteration in fluorescence intensity was observed when cations (even with a very high ionic concentration i.e. 1000μM) were added to the C-dots solution. Although Hg2+ showed a decrease in fluorescence intensity but that was not significant as compared to the quenching caused by Ag+ concentration. The presence of various cations did not affect the fluorescence recovery of C-dots-Ag+ complex upon addition of GSH.

Experimental results of cellular imaging using C-dots are shown in Fig. 9 . Rapid cellular uptake (within 2h) and multicolour emission of C-dots was observed in L929 cells. C-dots were primarily localised selectively in the cell cytoplasm and not in the nucleus. This is consistent with previously reported bioimaging studies where negatively charged C-dots (2–10nm) showed selective cytosolic localization [47,48] rather than in the nucleus. The internalization of C-dots takes place via non-endocytic pathway because of very small size and charge [49].

The C-dots were found to be highly biocompatible with mouse fibroblast L929 cells (Fig. 10 ). Cells showed the viability of more than 90% even at a higher concentration of C-dots i.e. 500μg/mL after 24h of treatment. Studies have reported cytotoxic nature of fluorescent carbon quantum dots at a concentration of 500μg/mL [47], where cell death occurred due to apoptosis and genotoxicity inside cells [50–52]. But in contrast, our synthesized C-dots were found to be highly biocompatible and can be safely used for biological applications. Jiang et al., 2015, reported Ag+ sensing where only mono-color (yellow) C-dots/Cysteine fluorescent sensor was developed [26], whilst in current report the C-dots are multi-fluorescent in nature, and therefore, gives an additional flexibility to develop an Ag+/GSH sensing probes in all three color ranges both in situ and inside the cells. In synthesis parameters, the C-dots were fabricated using a domestic microwave within a short time thus increasing reproducible synthesis reaction. The C-dots being biocompatible and highly photostable can be used further as “smart biosensors” for intracellular applications such as specific sensing of biomolecules inside the cells, which are pertinent to various disease states using intrinsic fluorescence properties of C-dots.

A simple prolonged microwave-assisted hydrothermal process for synthesis of N-doped C-dots using domestic microwave is demonstrated. Synthesized C-dots were used for dual sensing of silver ions and GSH based on fluorescence ‘turn-off’ and ‘turn-on’ mechanism, respectively. Effect of quality of water sample on fluorescence emission of C-dots was studied by using Milli-Q, purifier and tap water for sensing experiments. We anticipate that this ‘turn-off’ and ‘turn-on’ dual sensing method may have wide applicability for simultaneous detection of wide-range concentrations of silver and GSH in variety of analytes. C-dots also showed rapid cellular uptake with localization in the cell cytoplasm and cell viability assay confirmed high biocompatibility, therefore, it can be used for intracellular analyte detection without being detrimental to living cells or tissues. Thus, C-dots have an immense potential in biomedical sensing applications.
Authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Vivek Borse would like to acknowledge Department of Biotechnology, Government of India for the fellowship DBT-JRF/2012-13/248. Mukeshchand Thakur would like to acknowledge financial support from IYBA - Department of Biotechnology, Government of India. Authors are grateful to Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility (SAIF) and Industrial Research and Consultancy Centre (IRCC) for the Central Surface Analytical Facility (ESCA) for providing characterization facilities, Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering CLSM facility and Department of Metallurgical Engineering and Materials Science XRD facility at IIT Bombay, Mumbai.

Graphene quantum dots induce apoptosis, autophagy, and inflammatory response via p38 mitogen-activated protein kinase and nuclear factor-κB mediated signaling pathways in activated THP-1 macrophages

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mr. Vivek Borse has completed Graduation in Pharmacy with Gold medal from North Maharashtra University, Jalgaon, India; further he has completed Masters in Technology from NIPER, Mohali, India and currently a doctoral student in Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering at IIT Bombay, Mumbai, India. His research interest includes biosensor-based nanodiagnostics for infection detection.

Mr. Mukeshchand Thakur is currently Junior Research Fellow under Prof. Rohit Srivastava working on 2D materials, plasmonic nanomaterials and theranostics. He completed his Masters in Biotechnology (D. Y. Patil University, Mumbai) thereafter he has been actively working in the field of Nanobiotechnology since 2013 and contributed to more than 25 research articles in peer reviewed journal.

Ms. Sohini Sengupta has completed Bachelors in Technology (Biotechnology) from Heritage Institute of Technology, Kolkata, India in 2014. Further she received Masters degree in biomedical engineering from IIT Bombay, Mumbai, India in 2016. Currently she is working as a Software Engineer in Cytel Statistical Software & Services Pvt. Ltd., Pune, India.

Dr. Rohit Srivastava is Professor at the Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, IIT Bombay. His specialization lies in MEMS devices and nanoengineered biosensors. He has received many prestigious fellowships and awards such as DBT-IYBA 2009 award, INAE Young Engineer Award 2010, INAE Young Associateship, Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation GCE Phase 1, Tata Innovation Fellowship, Senior IYBA Fellowship, OPPI award, Grand Challenges Canada award, Vasvik award for Biological Sciences and Technology, Biotech Product and Process Development and Commercialization Award from DBT, Govt of India. He has also been elected a Member of the National Academy of Sciences, 2011. He has published more than 80 research articles in peer-reviewed journals. More than 20 patents have been filed from his lab. His lab is focusing on developing technologies that can be commercialized and brought to use for the common man in India.
